speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, my mother would take me to school every morning. And every morning or afternoon, or whatever time the teacher decided, I was thrown out of class. Because I would not--

speaker: interviewer
text: For not kneeling down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not kneeling down and not crossing myself. So I would go to-- oh, and Polish is another language. It's a different alphabet. It's a different-- my mother got beaten up in Russia for saying the word bee-let instead of bee-lette. It's some of the words are very similar. But there's still a difference. But reading and writing, it's a totally different story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started a new language. And Polish, Polish was my first language, really. And Russian came second, then, so that came sort of easier to me. And in no time at all, there was no problem with the Polish. But the reading and the writing, of course, I needed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because they kept throwing me out of school, I kept going to my mother. And my mother would bring me back the next morning. I would go to school. They'd throw me out, the same thing. That went on for a very long time. Until one day my mother decided this was really foolish. And there was a [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH], and she registered me in the Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now came a third alphabet, and Yiddish. And that was another language altogether. That was a foreign language. I started with that. Life sort of started to settle in. How did the Jewish kids accept you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those children were the same children as I. I didn't feel as an outsider. Because there was no [NON-ENGLISH] before. We were among the first ones to return. [NON-ENGLISH] before that was in German hands. When it turned into Polish hands, initially opened up, and it was Jewish children. So I wasn't the outsider. I wasn't the new kid on the block. I was the same as everybody else. My girlfriend, Rachel, continued with the Polish school. She did kneel. She did stay. Ironically enough, she wound up in Israel, and I came to Canada. Anyway, so we were in-- we had an apartment. My mother worked. I went to school. But we did start hearing about the antisemitism and the pogroms.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Newspaper, which wasn't so much. But there was a Jewish life emerging again in Poland, of the people who came from concentration camps, from hiding, from Russia, from different surrounding countries where they wanted to come back to, to their homeland. So a Jewish life started to emerge. And you can't help but hear all sorts of stories. Life was not what I guess my mother anticipated, she'd be able to pick up at a break, be able to pick up and start all over again. It was different. Number one, she was a woman alone. We hadn't heard from my father from the time we left Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although in Russia you were able to contact him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Russia we were corresponding. I sent him pictures. He sent me letters. So yes, even though he was very, very far away, letters did reach us and, and correspondence did go on. As a matter of fact, my mother had written to Dad that we would not leave Russia until he was freed. The reply came that he would feel a lot better knowing that we were back home, meaning Poland. But we didn't know what to expect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until that point, correspondence was going on. After that, there wasn't any. So, when these pogroms started and the danger again loomed for Jewish people, and a woman alone with a child, and this [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Zionistic movement reaching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people who had survived, Palestine, homeland; that sort of thing. And there were means and ways of reaching places from which you could go to Palestine. We left with a group of 30 people, [NON-ENGLISH], one night. Packed in the middle, and no forwarding addresses, with a group walking to a certain point. There was a cart for a certain point. After that, you had to continue walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we reached Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were there for a short time. No school, no-- just sitting, waiting, until the next lap of the journey could take place. Again, secretively, night-time with leaders from Israel, helping these people to reach certain collective points. Ours was eventually Germany. And we settled in-- on the US zone, Ulm, Ulm Alb-Donau. And in that city, there were five camps for displaced people. We were in Sedan Kaserne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother ever think of going elsewhere in Poland to be near her family, or your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we-- when we came back to Poland, the Red Cross was very, very instrumental in trying to find people and making connections of families. And there were none left from her family. There were none at that point left from my father's family. It was a tribe. It wasn't just a family. It was a tribe, uncles, aunts, great-aunts, cousins, great-cousins, you know, it was a-- it was a whole connecting chain of people that were related.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you learned since anything about their fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they all perished. They all perished. My father has a branch of the family that's in Israel. Mother's family, the only two were the-- now it's their offsprings, but a sister and a brother who immigrated to the United States before my mother was born. And my father's one brother, who was-- came here before the-- before the war. While in Germany, again through newspapers, my mother found my father's brother, Uncle Saul. He was, at that time, with a kibbutz in Germany, residing on a farm near Frankfurt. Now mind, the farm had originally belonged to [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a fabulous-- it was an incredible farm. And that's where the kibbutz took that over, until they-- it was their turn to go to Israel. But when we came, and I would imagine-- must have been at least two years since we had heard from my father. None of the other women, by the way, had heard from their men either. That my mother wrote a letter to my dad. She wrote the letter in Yiddish. She told him where we are, what has happened. And of course, she put on the return address, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] US zone, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: The letter came to the prison. They couldn't read the letter. However they did see US zone, Germany. And what better proof that did have that he was politically involved? So they started interrogations all over again. What has he got to do with the Russians? What has he got to do with the Germans? What has he got to do with the Americans? Proof that he was a spy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father never got the letter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He never had a chance to tell them what the letter was, nor did he have an inkling that this letter was sent to him by his wife who was now in Germany. From what he understood, we were in Warsaw back home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were in Germany. Now that must have been late '46, '47. I started school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were-- eventually, there were about 10,000 people in Ulm, maybe more. But among them there was quite a number of children of all ages, different countries, different backgrounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: What proportion of Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: And no common language. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, the purpose of these people in, in Germany was a concentrating point from which to take off for Palestine, new homeland. So Israel sent in [NON-ENGLISH], and they opened up schools. And school was conducted very, very intensely in Hebrew, everything in Hebrew. You were penalized for speaking any other language but Hebrew. And now you started [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] school in Hebrew. OK, alphabet, OK, I had from my Yiddish shula. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], you know, starting-- [LAUGHS] starting all over again. So-- but I felt pretty good about it. Because everybody else was in the same boat. They grouped children, not according to age, but according to knowledge. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I started my schooling in Russia, and they do have a very good school-- they really do-- I was with children a lot older than I. Because I was fairly good at school. And Hebrew, I liked. I also liked learning about my religion, my background, my history. And I also liked, of course, the continuation of algebra, of physics. And we used to live next door to the-- to the teacher. And I used to walk him back and forth to school. And he would-- he would talk about this-- these subjects. He was crazy about them. And he really instilled a great deal of liking for those subjects. And I have found that if you like the teacher, you do well in that subject. I really did like him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did well. And Hebrew was, was where I started. And I learned Hebrew. And in a three-year period, all the kids did. Our communication was in Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was released from prison. He didn't know that he was being released. He was just put in on a train. And when the train got to Warsaw, he saw that there were no more guards. And he looked around. He could come and go, and got off the train in Warsaw. Through the Red Cross, he found out that we were in [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was helped to get to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked for us. Yes. That was Hinda Denderowicz, Dina Denderowicz. And, and, and what happened to them? Nobody knows. You don't leave a forwarding address when you go that route.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was without funds. He was gaunt, clean-shaven. You know, they had no hair. And when you have a trade in your hands, you have-- you have a way of helping yourself, which he did. And he became a tailor. And he was working for a tailor store, factory, whatever have you. Now this was '47, maybe early '48. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm trying to tell two stories, [LAUGHS] you know, simultaneously.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was working in [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], in a store. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in Germany in Ulm. Now this is three years after the war. Most people began to look human. And most people in the camps, especially the young men, tried to advance in life, accumulate a little funds. Because that was the thing that helped them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one young man was smuggling the borders Poland-Germany, Poland-Germany. And while he was in Poland, decided to have a suit made. And he came into the store where my father worked. He looked a little odd, my father. Because as I said, he had just come out of Russia. And they struck up a conversation. [NON-ENGLISH], where does a Jew come from? And my father said, he was just released from, from Russia. Do you have family? He said, yes. He had a wife and a daughter. He doesn't know where they are. [NON-ENGLISH] was the last point. But he doesn't know where they are now. And as he was talking to him, he took out a picture from his breast pocket and showed him a picture of me that I had sent to him, still in Russia. And the young man looked at this picture, and he said, is her name Dina? And my father, said yes. He said, you know, I know her well, because she's a girlfriend to my niece and they live in Ulm, in Germany. And that's how we found my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father wrote right away, and it took some time. Because by now, it was not so easy. And the-- the Israeli brigade or what have you, was not so easily available to, to transport people and have the routes. You had to pay a guide. So between mom and dad working, they combined enough money to pay someone to guide my father over. And he joined us in-- he joined us-- at the end of-- no, beginning of '49, I guess, in the wintertime of '49 is when he joined us in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, his younger brother. You told me that the letter from your father arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it was [NON-ENGLISH], I suppose. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, 1947, '48 were not very good years for Israel, War of Independence. And we got, of course, news and in newsprint and in the news in general, what was going on with ships that were trying to land in Palestine still. Sending-- being sent back to Cyprus, conditions on Cyprus. And people who survived the war, and especially a woman alone with a child, I was not exactly ready to go and start [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a new war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in some ways, the papers were made out. And demands were made. And we had an opportunity to go to Argentina. And we were to take the afternoon train for Bremerhaven to board the ship for Argentina. And the letter from my father came that morning. And my mother said to me, what would you like to do? What do you think we should do? I don't know what kind of a morning it was. [BACKROUND NOISE] Maybe I was a little upset with her that day. Like my grandson says to me, it's not a good day for me. So, I said, I want my father. And of course we waited, and Dad came. And again, we had to start with papers, for-- for Canada, where his brother was from before. His younger brother was here by now too. And in August 14th, August 14th, we arrived in Canada via And that was it. We arrived in the Goldina Medina, Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: By now I was 12. We arrived on a Sunday. And on Monday, my father had a job at Tip-Top Tailors. It took my mother a little longer, till Wednesday. And she went to work for Paragon, I think, or some other company sewing purses. And this was August. So I waited a couple of weeks. And I started school. We started English. So I come into class, and I didn't understand. Miss Breckenridge was my teacher, and she was my son's teacher in grade 6 in parochial school afterwards. It was really something. But she said, alphabet. Alphabet is alphabet. That's no problem. Now it looked like the Polish alphabet to me. So I stood up and I said, [NON-ENGLISH], and the class really made fun of me. Oh, they made fun of me. But there were other-- there were three other children in my class with the same [NON-ENGLISH]. So we sort of stuck together. And when you're a youngster, you learn fairly quickly. And came English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was '49. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We rented a flat. I'm trying to think, you know, what-- how the sequence of events. I made friends with my cousin. I had a cousin. I had two uncles now. I had two aunts. I mean I'm-- the family was expanding. We had family reunions. It was bonding, because these were, you know, strangers really to me, that first Sunday. That was really something. Foods that I had never seen before, and I loved them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smoked fish and lox, and cisco, herring. [LAUGHS] Things were really great. Herring I knew, but not the kinds of herring that they had here. My aunt had prepared a real feast for when we landed. Well, we landed in, in Halifax. I've got to tell you this story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shortly before we were leaving for Canada, when we knew we were going to Canada, my parents decided I better learn English, maybe three weeks before. Now how much can you learn in three weeks, OK? So we land in Halifax. Everybody gets off the boat. There's a train waiting. Everybody rushing to-- you know, we also rushed. Well you took-- we went to a compartment. We sat down. There came a conductor, he said a few things in English. My parents said, what did he say? What did he say? [LAUGHS] I said, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave him the-- not passports, but the landings card. He gave it back. He was quite upset. It took a few tries, until somebody came along and explained that this compartment was not for immigrants. [LAUGHS] We had to leave and go to another one. It's like, quick, teach me English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Canada. It was-- it took us three days. And we, we got to Union Station. And there was a family welcome, and several taxis were waiting. And up University Avenue, which isn't exactly as it is now. In those days, they were big mansions, there was a large synagogue. A synagogue; that was really something to see, a synagogue. Had you ever been in a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, that was my, my first even sight of what a synagogue and what it means to-- or is supposed to mean to me, a synagogue was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have some Jewish education here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From a formal education in-- no. I did belong to the Hashomer Hatzair. I went back to the Hashomer Hatzair. Because that was my introduction to Zionism in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we missed talking about that. In Germany. You joined Hashomer Hatzair, and we're going to see some pictures of you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, my, my groups, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that when, when you're trying to explain something, it's one thing. But with words, come emotions. So now I was not yevreyka. Now I was one of. I mean-- and the whole idea, that whole spirit of being in Germany was we're going to Israel. We're going to Palestine. And Zionism was an introduction via which this, this, this whole thing is going to come about. And it was really great, a feeling that, that all of a sudden, you are no longer that outsider looking in. Now you are a part of-- everybody was Jewish in the camps. Everybody was a survivor of one sort or another. Everyone was going to fight for Israel and have an independent land.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you imagine your life in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, I didn't imagine. I was told exactly what my life was going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to fight. I was going to have training with, with guns and, and I was going to be a very-- I was going to be a very integral part of winning this, this war. And I was going to be part of Israel. And of course, [NON-ENGLISH], I mean there is a song. Do you know that song? Do you know-- [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know that song? That was the Hashomer Hatzair song. I mean that's exactly it. How good is to be at Hashomer Hatzair. In the morning you work. In the afternoon you eat. And in the evening you rest, and, you know, that's, that's it. So that was really pretty good.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came to Canada, not speaking the language, not knowing-- I mean, children my age were young ladies. I came with two big braids. I was a little girl in-- by the standards of what 12-year-olds in those days. So, I looked for my own. And I found on Melrose or on Beatrice Street. I think it was on Beatrice Street in some-- in a basement was the Hashomer Hatzair. We didn't jitterbug. We-- we danced the hora. And that was a great thing that I found them. We didn't have all the other things, you know, the trimmings that other kids did, you know, with the saddle shoes, with their jeans rolled up with the big pins, you know, with that little scarf with the thing. So I wore my father's pants with seven belts, you know, to keep it up. [LAUGHS] But that was-- that was nice. That was nice having that. And we, we stayed-- stayed together as friends over all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- at that point, did you still think you would go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, yes. You know, the Hashomer Hatzair and the Zionists, you know, yes. I was quite upset that I was not going to-- that I didn't wind up in Israel. Because that's the [NON-ENGLISH] spirit. I mean, that, that was what sustained. That's what we were. But don't forget very shortly after that, I met my future husband. I was 13 when my parents brought him home. And for the next three years he came home to our house, and he sat and watched the round television that we had like that with my father, while I sat and did homework. I mean, I didn't know anything about marriage or anything. That's the last thing on my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: But eventually, of course, that's what happened. And I married, and-- straight out of high school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I worked for a sum total of three-- six months, three months before the wedding. Then I got married. Three months after the wedding, I became pregnant. And my husband, by the old standards, a wife does not work. What will people say? I can't support her? So I stayed-- my parents and my husband bought a house together on Mount Royal. And I was supposedly the madam at home. But it wound up, I did the cooking, the washing, the cleaning for everybody. [LAUGHS] We're going to take a short break.
